narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.60 and a family from the middle income bracket, you've put real effort into building a strong profile for law school. Your plan to attend full-time also aligns with the path most students take, as over 92% choose that route. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your risk of failure at 45%—a threshold that unfortunately led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes candidates with lower predicted risks. The most influential factor pulling in your favor was actually your undergraduate GPA, which stands above the typical 3.21 average and strongly suggested to the model that you have the academic chops to succeed on the bar. Next in importance was your LSAT score of 28.50, well below the average of 36.70, which weighed against you by pushing the prediction toward a higher chance of failure. Your Year 1 school decile of 4, lower than the average 5.71, offered some positive counterbalance, indicating potential despite early challenges. Meanwhile, your Year 3 decile of 3, dipping below the typical 5.53, nudged the odds slightly toward failure, hinting at inconsistent performance later on. Being full-time helped a bit, as it's the norm, while your middle-income background had minimal sway. Compared to typical students, your above-average GPA is a standout strength that many would envy, but your LSAT and decile rankings lag behind the pack, tipping the scales. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a middle-class background—it's just how these key metrics stacked up. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score closer to or above average, strengthening your early and later school deciles through additional coursework or context in a new application, and highlighting any bar prep strategies. Many applicants in similar spots succeed on reapplication with targeted improvements—keep pushing, you've got a strong foundation to build on.
